questionHtml:
A molecule of citric acid, C6H8O7, is shown.

The equation for the first dissociation of citric acid in water is

C6H8O7 (aq) + H2O (l) ⇌ C6H7O7− (aq) + H3O+ (aq)

The value of the equilibrium constant for the first dissociation at 298 K is 5.01 × 10−4.

State, giving a reason, the strength of citric acid.
markschemeHtml:
weak acid AND partially dissociated
OR
weak acid AND equilibrium lies to left
OR
weak acid AND Kc/Ka<1 ✔

meta.title: The value of the equilibrium constant for the first dissociation at 298 K is 5.01 × 10−4. State, giving a reason, the strength of citric acid.

Reactivity 3.1.6—Strong and weak acids and bases differ in the extent of ionization. Recognize that acid–base equilibria lie in the direction of the weaker conjugate. and are strong acids, and group 1 hydroxides are strong bases.
Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far?
Reactivity 2.3—How far? The extent of chemical change

Reactivity 2.3.2—The equilibrium law describes how the equilibrium constant, K, can be determined from the stoichiometry of a reaction. Deduce the equilibrium constant expression from an equation for a homogeneous reaction.
